title: Limitations of Debye-Waller lattice temperature extraction under electronic excitation
authors: N. Medvedev, M. Kopecky, J. Chalupsky, L. Juha
abstract: Ultrafast diffraction is the cutting-edge technique to extract the atomic temperature at femtosecond timescales, and further related quantities - in particular, electron-phonon coupling strength at elevated electronic temperatures. The present work demonstrates limitations of such an analysis, emphasizing the importance of careful evaluation of the evolution of the Debye temperature. It is shown that, due to the sensitivity of the Debye-Waller analysis to this parameter, neglecting its changes under electronic excitation may lead to significant deviations of the atomic temperature extracted from its true values.
summary: This paper demonstrates that using the standard Debye-Waller analysis to extract atomic temperatures from ultrafast diffraction data is unreliable because it neglects the dynamic evolution of the Debye temperature. This oversight leads to significant errors in temperature estimation, especially during non-equilibrium phase transitions. The work highlights the need for more sophisticated analysis that accounts for changes in the interatomic potential and phonon spectrum.
main_problem: The inaccuracy of extracting transient atomic temperatures from ultrafast diffraction data when the Debye temperature evolves due to electronic excitation.
main_result: Neglecting changes in the Debye temperature during electronic excitation leads to significant errors in the extracted atomic temperature, with errors being most extreme during nonthermal phase transitions in non-metals.
method: Non-adiabatic molecular dynamics simulations using the XTANT-3 multiscale code, which combines Monte Carlo, Boltzmann collision integrals, and tight-binding-based molecular dynamics.
model_or_system: The study examines metals (Cu, Au) and non-metals (Si, Diamond) under ultrafast laser irradiation, focusing on far-from-equilibrium, two-temperature regimes.
key_observables: Atomic kinetic temperature (Ta), Debye-Waller temperature (Tdw), configurational temperature (Tconf), Debye temperature (thetaD), and diffraction peak intensities.
important_parameters: Femtosecond to picosecond timescales, absorbed dose, photon wavelength, and electron-phonon coupling strength.
main_assumptions: The standard Debye-Waller analysis assumes a constant Debye temperature, which the authors demonstrate is a flawed assumption under excitation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares simulated and experimental diffraction intensities; Figure 2 compares various temperature definitions across materials; Figure 3 shows the evolution of the Debye temperature; Figure 4 highlights the divergence between Tdw and Ta in Cu.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of temperature extraction errors, validates the XTANT-3 simulation model against experimental data, demonstrates the evolution of the Debye temperature under excitation, and quantifies the resulting errors in temperature and electron-phonon coupling estimation.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Diffraction peak intensities after ultrafast irradiation. (a) Copper irradiated with λ=400 nm wavelength pulse, τ=130 fs FWHM duration, D=0.83 eV/atom absorbed dose; (b) Gold irradiated with λ=400 nm, τ=130 fs, D=0.69 eV/atom. Lines are simulated with XTANT-3, and circles are experimental data for copper [26] and gold [27].

Figure 2. Evolution of the atomic temperature after ultrafast irradiation. (a) Copper irradiated with λ=400 nm wavelength pulse, τ=130 fs FWHM duration, D=0.83 eV/atom absorbed dose; (b) Gold irradiated with λ=400 nm, τ=130 fs, D=0.69

Figure 3. Evolution of the Debye temperature after ultrafast irradiation, extracted from the various diffraction peaks (denoted in the legends), simulated with XTANT-3. (a) Copper irradiated with λ=400 nm wavelength pulse, τ=130 fs FWHM duration, D=0.83 eV/atom absorbed dose; (b) Gold irradiated with λ=400 nm, τ=130 fs, D=0.69 eV/atom; (c) Silicon irradiated with ħω=92 eV photon energy, τ=10 fs, D=1.1 eV/atom; (d) Diamond irradiated with ħω=92 eV, τ=10 fs, D=1.5 eV/atom.

Figure 4. Evolution of the atomic temperature in time after ultrafast irradiation of copper with a 400 nm wavelength pulse, 130 fs FWHM duration, 0.83 eV/atom absorbed dose. Solid line is calculated atomic temperature, Ta; dashed lines are atomic temperature extracted from the simulated diffraction peaks via Debye-Waller analysis of various diffraction peaks, TDW, assuming constant Debye temperature. Red circles are the Debye-Waller atomic temperature extracted from experimentally measured diffraction peaks [13].

Figure 5. Evolution of the electronic and atomic temperatures after ultrafast irradiation. (a) Copper irradiated with 400 nm wavelength pulse, 130 fs FWHM duration, 0.83 eV/atom absorbed dose; (b) Gold irradiated with 400 nm wavelength pulse, 130 fs FWHM duration, 0.69 eV/atom absorbed dose; (c) Silicon irradiated with 92 eV photon energy pulse, 10 fs FWHM duration, 1.1 eV/atom absorbed dose; (d) Diamond irradiated with 92 eV photon energy pulse, 10 fs FWHM duration, 1.5 eV/atom absorbed dose.
